overall_summary: Eolian, an independent developer of both generation and large load, urges FERC to require nationwide tariff reforms that integrate load and generation interconnection studies, offer non-firm transmission services to flexible loads (not just co-located ones), and create incentives for generators to site assets where they open headroom for load. Eolian supports the Commission acting through show cause orders or rulemaking but argues that recent SPP and PJM initiatives fall short and must be supplemented by these broader reforms.

quotes.text: we have not encountered any transmission provider that currently has a process which (i) adequately integrates or coordinates the interconnection of supply resources and load, (ii) provides developers of supply resources such as Eolian with adequate incentive to locate generation and storage assets in a manner that opens additional system headroom for load interconnection, or (iii) offers the full range of transmission service options necessary to allow supply and load to coordinate to optimally use the existing system and reduce transmission infrastructure needs.
quotes.concern: No transmission provider today has a process that coordinates load and generation interconnection, incentivizes efficient siting, or offers the full range of needed transmission services.

quotes.text: The lack of tariff provisions to afford all three elements results in unjust and unreasonable or unduly discriminatory rates by exacerbating the reliability and affordability impacts of large load growth, while impeding speed-to-power for new large loads.
quotes.concern: Missing tariff provisions create unjust rates and slow speed-to-power for large loads.

quotes.text: the Commission can require load and generation study coordination even if it decides against asserting jurisdiction over load interconnection by requiring transmission providers to coordinate in generation interconnection study processes with the relevant transmission owners or electric distribution companies conducting load interconnection studies.
quotes.concern: FERC can mandate coordinated study processes without asserting load interconnection jurisdiction.

quotes.text: the Commission should require consistent standards for when and how load is included in generator interconnection studies.
quotes.concern: Uniform standards are needed for reflecting load in generator interconnection studies.

quotes.text: The relevant interconnection process must provide a path for permanent interconnection service. Temporary frameworks that hinge on subsequent processing through a standard queue create commercial risks that are likely to inhibit widespread use of these interconnection pathways.
quotes.concern: Temporary interconnection service frameworks create unworkable commercial risk; permanent service is needed.

quotes.text: The Commission must require transmission providers to offer transmission services that match the ability of loads to use the capabilities of adjacent generation, including non-firm and reduced service options. Such service must allow the load to take service when the grid is unconstrained and the supply resource is not available (including when a battery resource is charging) while electing to curtail when the grid is constrained.
quotes.concern: Non-firm and reduced transmission service options must cover flexible loads relying on adjacent generation, not just co-located resources.

quotes.text: the Commission should explicitly direct transmission providers to make the non-firm services available to all loads willing and able to limit withdrawals from the transmission system rather than limiting availability only to co-located loads.
quotes.concern: Non-firm service should extend to all flexible loads, not only those co-located with generation.

quotes.text: whatever non-firm transmission services that the Commission directs transmission providers to offer in this proceeding must be designed to allow for broad availability of non-firm service during non-emergency conditions and not only during associated generator outages.
quotes.concern: Non-firm service must be available in normal (non-emergency) operating conditions, not only during generator outages.

quotes.text: the Commission must require additional reforms beyond recently approved initiatives that some regions have enacted to ensure timely and affordable integration of large loads. While recent reforms in Southwest Power Pool (SPP) and PJM Interconnection, LLC (PJM) are positive steps, these reforms fall short of what is needed to fully address the large load interconnection challenges identified in the ANOPR.
quotes.concern: Recent SPP and PJM reforms are insufficient; additional nationwide reforms are required.

quotes.text: HILLGA and CHILLS are not permanent service options, interconnection of both load and generation via this process remains subject to network upgrades that may be subsequently identified in the load and generation's permanent interconnection study processes. And such permanent interconnection study process, which occurs under SPP's Consolidated Planning Process, may be years after the facilities are operational. This creates potentially unworkable commercial risks for both load and generation that will inhibit use of these processes
quotes.concern: SPP CHILLS/HILLGA processes are time-limited and expose projects to late-stage network upgrade costs, making them commercially unworkable.

quotes.text: PJM's Phase II study results assigned $158 million in network upgrade costs to Matta Grid, largely driven by a grid constraint 25 miles from both the Matta Grid project and the proposed Amazon data center. A Timmons Group study that Eolian commissioned demonstrated that, had PJM included even a very conservative 150 MW of the 770 MW of Amazon load in its Phase II study for Transition Cycle 1, the study results would have shown over $100 million less in system reinforcements assigned to the Matta Grid project.
quotes.concern: Excluding nearby load from generator interconnection studies inflated Matta Grid's upgrade costs by over $100 million.

quotes.text: utilities may provide for outcomes that maximize their own return on equity while triggering the construction of infrastructure that would not be needed were the process to more accurately reflect planned system additions.
quotes.concern: Utility discretion over what load to include in study models can favor infrastructure build-out that maximizes utility returns.

quotes.text: Under PJM's current rules, however, there is little incentive for developers such as Eolian to locate assets to provide these system benefits. Developers do not get any credit or earn any additional revenues for opening additional system capacity.
quotes.concern: Generators that strategically site assets to open system headroom receive no credit or revenue under current rules.

quotes.text: the generation interconnection process afforded generators the ability to generate 'tags' representing the transmission capacity that the proposed generation opens. Such a transmission capacity tag could then be used in the load interconnection process, allowing load to contract with generation to support development that enables development to move forward that is currently on hold.
quotes.concern: A transmission capacity tag mechanism would let generators monetize headroom they create and allow loads to contract for it.

bins.name: Coordinated Load-Generation Studies
bins.description: Eolian argues that load and generation interconnection studies must be coordinated, with consistent standards for when and how load is reflected in generator studies. Real-world examples from PJM and PacifiCorp show that omitting nearby load from generator studies inflates network upgrade costs by tens to hundreds of millions of dollars and can cancel viable projects.

bins.name: Non-Firm Service for Flexible Loads
bins.description: Eolian supports non-firm and curtailable transmission service options for loads that commit to limit withdrawals during grid constraints, whether co-located or merely adjacent to supply. It asks that such service be available during normal (non-emergency) conditions and be offered on a permanent rather than time-limited basis.

bins.name: Co-location and Adjacent Arrangements
bins.description: Eolian supports extending co-location-style benefits to loads served by adjacent (electrically proximate) generation, not only loads at the same point of interconnection. It argues that PJM's Co-Location Order is a useful starting point but must be broadened to cover adjacent arrangements.

bins.name: Incentives for Proximate Generation Siting
bins.description: Eolian argues that generators siting assets to open headroom for nearby load get no credit under current rules, removing the incentive to locate efficiently. It proposes a transmission capacity tag mechanism through which generators could monetize the headroom they create and loads could contract for it.

bins.name: Reducing Unnecessary Network Upgrade Costs
bins.description: Eolian contends that uncoordinated studies trigger network upgrade costs that would not arise under integrated processes. SPP's temporary CHILLS and HILLGA programs leave projects exposed to late-stage upgrade assignments, creating commercial risk for both load and generation developers.

bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Over Load Interconnection
bins.description: Eolian notes that FERC can require coordinated study processes regardless of whether it asserts direct jurisdiction over load interconnection, by directing transmission providers to coordinate with utilities or distribution companies conducting load studies.

bins.name: Joint Load-Generation Study Process
bins.description: Eolian endorses a joint or coordinated study process and asks at minimum that generators be able to nominate associated loads for inclusion in their interconnection study, with consistent standards applied across all transmission providers.

bins.name: Network Upgrade Cost Assignment
bins.description: Eolian presents the Matta Grid example to show that excluding load from generator studies inflated network upgrade cost assignments by over $100 million, and argues that reformed study processes would reduce unjustified upgrade costs.

bins.name: PJM Study and Co-location Reforms
bins.description: Eolian supports the PJM Co-Location Order as an important step but identifies two gaps: study models do not account for flexible load and generation arrangements, and non-firm services are limited to co-located loads rather than adjacent ones. The Matta Grid example shows how PJM's current process caused $158 million in study-assigned upgrade costs by omitting nearby load.

bins.name: SPP CHILLS and HILLGA Limitations
bins.description: Eolian supports SPP's development of CHILLS and HILLGA but argues that their time-limited, non-permanent nature exposes both load and generation to unquantifiable curtailment risk and subsequent network upgrade exposure, making them commercially unworkable for most developers.

bins.name: Nationwide Tariff Reform Required
bins.description: Eolian argues that deficient tariff provisions exist in every region it operates in, not just high-growth areas, and that FERC must require nationwide reforms regardless of the procedural vehicle (rulemaking or show cause orders).

bins.name: Consistent Load Modeling Standards
bins.description: Eolian asks FERC to require uniform, transparent standards for when and how load additions are included in generator interconnection studies, to prevent utilities from exercising discretion in ways that omit known planned loads.

bins.name: Permanent vs. Temporary Interconnection Service
bins.description: Eolian argues that interconnection pathways must offer permanent service rights. Temporary frameworks that require later re-processing through a standard queue create commercial risks that will deter developers from using them.

bins.name: Non-Firm Service in Normal Conditions
bins.description: Eolian asks that non-firm transmission service be available broadly during non-emergency, non-constrained conditions, not only when the associated generator is offline, to give flexible loads meaningful curtailment-based service.

bins.name: Reforms Must Go Beyond Existing Initiatives
bins.description: Eolian acknowledges recent regional reforms as positive steps but argues they fall short; the Commission must require additional, nationwide reforms to fully address the large load interconnection challenge identified in the ANOPR.

bins.name: Utility Incentives to Overbuild Infrastructure
bins.description: Eolian raises concern that utilities control what load and infrastructure appears in interconnection study models, and their return-on-equity incentives may lead them to omit load data in ways that trigger more infrastructure construction.

bins.name: Incentives for Efficient Generator Siting
bins.description: Eolian argues that generators currently receive no credit for locating assets where they would open system capacity for load, removing the incentive to site efficiently. Reforms must create a mechanism for generators to earn revenue for the headroom they create.

bins.name: Transmission Capacity Tag Mechanism
bins.description: Eolian proposes that the generator interconnection process issue tags representing the transmission capacity a project opens, which loads could then use to contract with generation and move stalled projects forward.
